observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (1) direct the Department Heads concerned to immediately prepare a consolidated report on all management actions taken relative to the audit observations and recommendations included in the Annual Audit Report for CY 2017 using the prescribed AAPSI Form; and (2) henceforth, strictly adhere to the prescribed period for the submission of the AAPSI as provided under the GAA.
observations.managementAction: None
observations.reason:
No AAPSI for the audit recommendations cited in the CY 2017 AAR was submitted to the Audit Team.

According to the Management, this was due to the non-compliance of the previous administration.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (a) submit a reasonable explanation for failure to deposit collections totaling ₱7,599,456.38 despite having been to the bank on December 16, 2019; (b) deposit the amount of ₱7,599,456.38 to the AGDB and prepare a report showing full accounting of the Cash in Local Treasury Account; and (c) henceforth, deposit all municipal collections intact strictly in accordance with Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 whenever there is opportunity to transact with the AGDB.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (a) submit written explanation for continuously not using the proper forms in reporting bank deposits for CY 2019; and (b) henceforth, report all bank deposits in Section B of the RCD in accordance with Section 32, Volume I and Section 49, Volume II of the NGAS Manual in order to check proper matching of collections and deposits.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Treasurer immediately submit to the Municipal Accountant (i) all the paid vouchers and payrolls; (ii) copy of validated deposit slips; and (iii) other relevant documents to determine the nature and purpose of unrecorded transactions reflected in the bank statements; and
Municipal Accountant (i) send a letter to the depository bank requesting for a copy of all the bank debit and credit memoranda for CY 2019; and (ii) exert more effort in obtaining sufficient documents to be used as basis for verification and recording of bank transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (a) exert efforts to update all cashbooks, reconcile them with the subsidiary accounting records and then submit a certified true copy to the Audit Team; and (b) henceforth, strictly observe daily updating of all cashbooks and monthly reconciliation with accounting records in accordance with Sections 26 and 27 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume II, and Section 63-F of the LTOM.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor, through the Inventory Committee, require all the Department Heads to conduct a complete physical count of all PPE under their respective offices and submit the report thereon to the Inventory Committee for verification and reconciliation with the accounting records; and
Municipal Treasurer prepare Property Cards per category of PPE and henceforth maintain the same in accordance with Section 119 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Treasurer (a) submit a reasonable explanation for signing the checks and releasing payment for DVs and payrolls totaling ₱6,167,464.34 despite lack of certification as to availability of funds and the LCE’s approval; and (b) henceforth, ensure that no disbursement of government funds shall be made without the complete certification and approval of proper officials pursuant to Section 4.5 of PD No. 1445 and Section 344 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, henceforth, observe utmost diligence in recording transactions to ensure strict adherence with the Revised Chart of Accounts prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 as well as the fair presentation of account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Treasurer, being the officer accountable for the cash advance, submit: (i) valid AIR and ARE to document acceptance and inspection of and accountability for the Blood Chemistry Analyzer; (ii) certification from the Municipal Health Officer that the Blood Chemistry Analyzer purchased may be accepted as a reasonable alternative for the equipment supposedly  requested; and (iii) written justification for drawing the cash advance and authorizing the former Municipal Mayor to encash the check despite the questionable nature of the said cash advance; Otherwise, refund the amount of ₱425,500.00 to settle the cash advance;
Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Accountant, ensure the immediate settlement of the cash advance by issuing a demand letter to the Accountable Officer, copy furnished the Audit Team; and
Municipal Vice-Mayor/then Mayor submit a satisfactory written explanation why he allowed the cash advance to be transferred to him and then performed the disbursing function.

We also recommended that, henceforth, the (i) requesting officials observe the rules and regulations in the preparation of PR; (ii) BAC Chairperson and members be mindful of their duties and responsibilities in the procurement of goods in accordance with the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; (iii) local officials concerned see that the PO contain all the basic requirements pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996; and (iv) accountable officers observe strictly the guidance in the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances as provided under COA Circular No. 97-002, as amended.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (a) submit a satisfactory written explanation for the delayed reporting of community tax collections totaling ₱203,908.00; (b) impose proper segregation of duties and functions in the Municipal Treasury Office in accordance with Section 50 of the GAAM Volume III; and (c) henceforth, ensure prompt reporting of all municipal collections at the close of each working day in accordance with Section 29 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the:
BAC (i) submit a reasonable explanation for failure to disqualify the bidders who submitted quotations higher than the ABC, and (ii) henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its IRR to ensure that all purchases of the Municipality go through the correct procurement process; and
Municipal Accountant, henceforth, ensure that all purchases are duly supported with complete and proper documentation, including the duly signed ABC, APP or excerpts thereof showing the subject goods to be procured, all the relevant BAC resolutions, duly accomplished RFQs containing complete information, abstract of bids/quotation and evaluation report on bids/price quotations, among others.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the Provincial Government of Palawan to submit the Report of Checks Issued and Report of Disbursement pertaining to the remaining ₱75,000.00; and (b) based on the Report of Checks Issued and Report of Disbursement, issue demand letter requesting for the return of any unutilized balance.

The Management has not yet provided a reply or comment to this audit observation.

We recommended that the:
MPDC prepare a complete monitoring report on the status of implementation of government PPAs as of December 31, 2019, including other programs and activities under the General Fund, projects under the 20% DF continuing appropriations and PPAs funded by national government agencies and other fund sources; and
Municipal Engineer assist the MPDC in monitoring the status of implementation of all infrastructure projects of the Municipality, regardless of fund source.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) propose to the Sangguniang Bayan the adoption of the LEE Manual as recommended under LBC No. 111 dated June 10, 2016; (b) assign or designate the relevant local officials who will be responsible in planning for and implementing the new LEEs; and (c) enjoin the said officials to conduct an evaluation on the operations of the municipal public market, slaughterhouse, hospital, cemetery and commercial/office spaces in accordance with Section 4 of the LEE Manual.

We recommended that the:
MPDC intensify the monitoring and evaluation of the implementation of development projects and henceforth submit quarterly reports on the same with recommendations for management actions on projects with unsatisfactory work accomplishments;
Municipal Engineer henceforth conduct regular inspection of all municipal public works and submit a monthly report on the status of implementation of all infrastructure projects; and
Municipal Mayor ensure the optimal utilization of the 20% DF to achieve the socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the Municipality.

We recommended that the:
MPDC formulate integrated economic, social, physical and other developmental plans and policies for consideration of the Municipal Development Council in coming up with a comprehensive local development plan that is compliant with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1;
Municipal Development Council exercise prudence in the formulation of the development plans and public investment programs to ensure that the 20% DF is used solely for the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes that partake the nature of investment or capital outlay; and
Municipal Accountant, henceforth, ensure that all disbursements out of the 20% DF are (i) supported with a duly approved Program of Work or other documentation that would establish the nature of the expenditure as investment or capital outlay and (ii) not among the list of expenditure items which are prohibited under Section 4.0 of the same JMC.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer initiate the transfer of the amount of ₱627,964.00 from the General Fund bank account to the STF depository account for LDRRMF and then submit the necessary documents to the Municipal Accountant for recording purposes.

We recommended that the:
MDRRMO (i) submit a summary report on the distribution of relief goods and supplies for CY 2019, duly supported with distribution forms duly acknowledged by the beneficiaries; and (ii) initiate the preparation of a supplemental investment plan for the utilization of the unexpended balance of prior years’ LDRRMF;
Municipal Accountant (i) submit an explanation why the payments for relief goods totaling ₱133,650.00 were drawn from the STF despite lack of investment plan for unexpended prior years’ LDRRMF; (ii) prepare and maintain Supplies Ledger Cards for all relief goods and supplies; and (iii) draw the necessary journal entries to account for the remaining relief goods and supplies, if any; and
Municipal Treasurer, as the Property and Supply Officer, prepare Stock Cards for all inventory items and henceforth observe proper monitoring of acquisition and distribution of relief goods and supplies.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer (a) submit a written explanation on why taxes withheld were not promptly remitted intact to the BIR; (b) cause the immediate remittance of all taxes withheld to the BIR to avoid possible interests, penalties and surcharges for late payments; and (c) ensure, henceforth, that taxes withheld are remitted intact and on the prescribed period pursuant to BIR Revenue Regulation No. 2-98 dated April 17, 1998, as amended.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Accountant prepare an analysis of the unremitted balance of the Due to PhilHealth account, reconcile billings and remittances and effect necessary adjustments and corrections, as appropriate; and
Municipal Treasurer, (i) based on the analysis prepared by the Municipal Accountant, remit to the PhilHealth all contributions from prior periods so that only those from the preceding month shall remain as balance of Due to PhilHealth account; and (ii) henceforth, ensure the prompt remittance of PhilHealth contributions within the period prescribed under PhilHealth Circular No. 0001, s. 2014 to avoid incurrence of unnecessary interest and penalties.

We recommended that the
Municipal Accountant prepare an analysis of the unremitted balance of the Due to GSIS account, reconcile billings and remittances and effect necessary adjustments and corrections, as appropriate; and
Municipal Treasurer, (i) based on the analysis prepared by the Municipal Accountant, remit to the GSIS all contributions and/or loan amortizations from prior periods so that only those from the preceding month shall remain as the balance of Due to GSIS account; and (ii) henceforth, ensure that GSIS contributions and loan amortization are remitted intact within the prescribed period under RA No. 8291 for the welfare of the employees and to avoid incurrence of unnecessary interest and penalties.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconstruct the general ledger for the months of January to June 2018 in accordance with the NGAS Manual, complete with descriptions and cross-references to appropriate journals. Validate the account balances as of June 30, 2018 by ensuring that all entries posted in the general ledger are substantiated with complete and proper supporting documents.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant henceforth prepare separate JEV for each RCD under GF and SEF in accordance with Section 34 of the NGAS Manual Volume I to ensure recording of financial transactions in the journals in time sequence.


We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer henceforth report all bank deposits in the RCD in accordance with Section 32, Volume I and Section 49, Volume II of the NGAS Manual in order to check proper matching of collections and deposits.


We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant cause the immediate reconciliation of the monthly balances of cashbooks with the subsidiary accounting records and henceforth, observe strict adherence to Section 63-F of the LTOM.


We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:

Submit a reasonable explanation on: (i) the incorrect sequence of transactions in her cashbooks; (ii) the timing differences between actual and recorded dates; and (iii) errors on the computation of cashbook balances; and

Henceforth observe strict compliance with Section 29 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I, on the daily reporting of collections and Sections 26 and 27 on the daily updating and balancing of cashbooks.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:

submit a reasonable explanation for failure to deposit all collections intact during CY 2018;

deposit all the cash under her custody to the AGDB and prepare a report showing full accounting of the Cash in Local Treasury Account; and

henceforth, strictly deposit all municipal collections intact in accordance with Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 whenever there is opportunity to transact with the AGDB.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor designate a Disbursing Officer who shall be in charge of the cash disbursements through regular or special cash advances drawn in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer henceforth refrain from making cash payments out of the Cash in Local Treasury.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

impose strictly the provision requiring all accountable officers to fully and promptly liquidate their cash advances as soon as the purpose for which it was given has been served or at the end of each year, whichever comes first;

cause the issuance of demand letters to all accountable officers with unliquidated cash advances as of December 31, 2018; and

henceforth: (i) require accountable officers to attach to the Liquidation Report a photocopy of the official receipt as proof of refund of unexpended balance of their cash advance; and (ii) make sure that no additional cash advance is allowed to any official or employee unless the previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made.
observations.managementAction: All cash advances granted in CY 2018 have already been liquidated as of December 31, 2019.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:

Municipal Treasurer: (i) obtain a copy of the Abstract of RPT Collections for RPT/SET shares received from the Provincial Government of Palawan and prepare a report on the correct RPT and SET income received and earned therefrom; and (ii) henceforth provide sufficient documentation to the Accounting Office upon submission of reports on collection to serve as basis for proper recording of income, discounts and penalties; and

Municipal Accountant: (i) prepare journal entries to adjust the RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred RPT/SET Income accounts to their correct balances; and (ii) henceforth observe strict adherence with Section 111 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2015-009, particularly by maintaining separate ledger accounts for discounts and penalties on RPT/SET and observing proper recording procedures for the same.
observations.managementAction:
The Office of the Treasurer thru Ms. Jasmine Cervancia observed the practice of maintaining an RPT abstract and regularly submitting the necessary documents to the Accounting Office, especially those pertaining to RPT shares from the Provincial Government.

The Accounting Office adopted the correct accounting treatment for RPT and SET related accounts in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Collecting Officer, Ms. Jasmine V. Cervancia:

submit satisfactory explanation on the delayed remittance of RPT collections totaling ₱470,800.78;

impose strict policies and control measures to ensure prompt remittance of collections pursuant to Section 26 of COA Circular No. 92-382; and

cause the immediate submission of CRAAF for all the months of CY 2018 and henceforth, ensure timely preparation and submission of CRAAF in accordance with Section 37 of the same COA Circular.
observations.managementAction:
The Collecting Officer had already submitted her explanation on the delayed remittance of her RPT collections. The Management imposed the FIFO Policy for accountable forms.
The Municipal Treasurer submitted the CRAAF for CY 2018.

The Sangguniang Panlalawigan of Palawan passed a Provincial Ordinance amending the rules on granting of RPT discounts for “advance” and “prompt” payments and the same has been disseminated during barangay assembly. The Municipality applied the amended rules for tax collections made in CY 2019.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer failed to collect the under-collection of RPT in CY 2018 totaling ₱170,653.07.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MPDC cause the immediate issuance of a tracer letter to the Provincial Government of Palawan (PGP) to follow-up on the request to return the unexpended fund transfers, along with a copy of the Municipal Mayor’s request letter dated September 11, 2018.

observations.reason: The amount of ₱75,000.00 still remained unliquidated as of December 31, 2019.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:

Municipal Mayor submit an explanation on why he allowed the procurement of fuel, oil and lubricants from Mr. Raymond L. Ong, considering their relation to each other, despite the prohibition for the same as provided under Section 47.1 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184;

Municipal Accountant (i) review the transactions identified herein and henceforth, ensure that no disbursements are approved unless duly and completely supported with the necessary documents, especially those evidencing the procurement activities conducted by the BAC; and (ii) cause the submission of lacking supporting documents to establish the validity and propriety of the disbursements; and

The Municipal Treasurer refrain from signing and issuing payments for DVs with clear and noticeable deficiencies.

In CY 2019, the Municipality no longer procured fuel from Mr. Raymond Ong. The Management prospectively imposed stringent measures against uncertain transactions.
No more cash advance was drawn for confidential fund in CY 2019.
observations.reason: The Management failed to submit justification on the validity of cash advances, certification from PNP, duly reconciled and approved POP with complete references to the AIP, and MPOC Resolution declaring approval by two-thirds of its members on the release and use of CF.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:

Municipal Engineer cause the immediate submission of: (i) the results of preliminary design and construction studies in connection with the design and build scheme; (ii) a summary report of all inspections conducted on the projects, duly supported with inspection reports, statements of time elapsed and pictures of work items before, during and after construction; (iii) duly approved detailed program of works prepared by the contractor in accordance with Section 13.3, Annex “G” of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and (iv) a reasonable explanation for the delay in the projects’ completion;
 
BAC Chairperson: (i) submit a reasonable explanation for failure to consider during the Bid Evaluation and Post Qualification Evaluation the Preliminary Conceptual Design Plans, Design and Construction Methods, and Value Engineering Analysis as part of the requirements to be included in the bidder’s technical proposals; and (ii) cause the immediate submission of the Minutes of Pre-Procurement Conference, Pre-Bid Conference and Bid Opening; and

Municipal Accountant: (i) cause the immediate submission of a copy of the MOA or Trust Agreement on the Salintubig Program; and (ii) henceforth, ensure that all disbursements are supported with complete documentation pursuant to Section 4.6 of P.D. No. 1445.

The projects remained unfinished as of December 31, 2019, as discussed under Audit Observation No. 4 in this AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor compel the MDRRMO to prepare and submit the LDRRMFIP as required under COA Circular No. 2012-002.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month as prescribed under the same COA Circular.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO prepared the LDRRMFIP using the correct format for CY 2020. The Municipal Accountant submitted the monthly report on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF.
observations.reason: The MDRRMO still failed to prepare the LDRRMFIP for CYs 2018 and 2019 using the correct format.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation for the Municipal Mayor to require the Inventory Committee to conduct an immediate physical count of all its PPE to determine the actual existence and proper valuation of the same, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.  The Inventory Committee may coordinate and request the assistance of the department heads to conduct inventory of all properties under their respective offices and submit the inventory report to the committee for validation and verification.

We also reiterated our recommendation that the ICO-Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant should create, update and maintain the required property cards and ledger cards.  Moreover, cause the reconciliation of results of the actual count with the property and accounting records.  All discrepancies between physical and book inventories must be investigated, adjusted and reconciled immediately.

Furthermore, we reiterated that the physical count of PPE should be undertaken annually by the Inventory Committee and the corresponding RPCPPE be submitted to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year as required under Section 124 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume I.
observations.managementAction: According to the ICO-Municipal Treasurer, partial physical count of PPE has already commenced.
observations.reason: No copy of RPCPPE, whether partial or complete, has been submitted to the Audit Team. Moreover, property cards were still not updated by the ICO-Municipal Treasurer.
AAPSI for CY 2017 and 2018 were still not submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:

Henceforth, we recommended strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.
observations.reason: Report on PPA for CY 2017 and 2018 were still not submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:

Henceforth, we recommended strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

observations.reason: The same deficiency was observed in CY 2018 and reiterated on this AAR.

1.2 Collections totaling ₱20,074,426.77 were accounted for without specific journal
entries per individual Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) contrary to Section
34 of the NGAS Manual Volume I and Section 2 of the NGAS Manual Volume II
thus, accountabilities of collectors for collections and accountable forms could not
be validated and the propriety and accuracy of journal entries made could not be
verified.
Cash Receipts Journals (CRJ) under the General Fund (GF) and Special
Education Fund (SEF) disclosed the following collections for CY 2018:

The Accounting Office recorded these transactions based on a Summary of
Collections submitted monthly by the Treasurer. The Summary of Collections is a
worksheet showing the total monthly collections for all funds combined, divided
according to different revenue accounts and then distributed to national, provincial,
municipal and barangay levels of local government. It does not provide references to
individual RCDs issued during the month. It also does not show how the apportionment
of collections into the revenue accounts were computed.
Each monthly Summary of Collections was accompanied with a list of RCDs,
which we found to be misleading because: (a) it was referred to as an RCD itself although
it is actually just a list of the RCDs providing their dates, control numbers and total
amount collected; (b) it grouped the different RCDs according to fund but does not
indicate which particular fund; and (c) the column for RCD control number is labeled as
“voucher no.” instead of “RCD No.”. Furthermore, we found no relevance of the said list
to the Summary of Collections except that their grand totals were supposed to be equal.
We have observed that for all collections under GF and SEF, the Accounting
Office did not prepare a separate Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) for each RCD.
Accounting for collections was done on a monthly basis and JEVs were prepared
according to the nature of collections.
Under the GF, collections were categorized into groups of similar revenue sources
such as (a) real property taxes, (b) community taxes, (c) supervision, clearance and
verification fees (d) permit, certification and registration fees and (e) receipts from rents,
slaughterhouse and market. Only one JEV was prepared for each of these groups per
month, regardless of the number of RCDs submitted.
Under SEF, only one JEV was being prepared per month and that is to account for
the additional tax on real property.
The CRJ was prepared based on these JEVs and accordingly, no separate journal
entry was being recorded for each RCD. Attached thereto was the Summary of
Collections and the aforementioned list of RCDs. No supporting computation was
provided to show in detail how the data from different RCDs were consolidated.
The foregoing method of accounting for collections is contrary to Section 34 of
the NGAS Manual Volume I, which provides:
33
Accounting for Collections and Deposits. – The Accountant shall
determine the account classification of the collections covered by the RCD
and the supporting papers submitted by the Treasurer/Cashier and shall
accomplish the Journal Entry Voucher. XXX
Section 35 of the same Manual provides step-by-step procedures to be followed
for the collection process. It was stated thereon that the Treasurer shall forward the RCD
to the Accounting Unit and the Accountant shall prepare a JEV. This means that the
Accountant shall prepare a separate JEV for each RCD submitted during the month. The
RCD being referred to is the individual report prepared by each accountable officer using
the format prescribed under Annex 41 of the NGAS Manual Volume II and not monthly
summaries prepared by the Treasurer.
Non-preparation of a separate JEV per RCD defeated the primary function of
journals, which is to serve as a record of financial transactions “in time sequence”.
Section 2 of the NGAS Manual Volume II, provides that:
Books of original entry or journals shall be used to record in time
sequence, financial transactions and information presented in duly certified
and approved accounting documents. (Underscoring supplied)
Furthermore, the CRJ was supposed to provide complete information on
collections, including the RCD control number and name of accountable officer. This
information could not be found on the CRJs prepared by the Accounting Office, contrary
to Section 5 of the NGAS Manual Volume II which provides:
Cash Receipts Journal (CRJ). – This journal is used to record all
collections and deposits reported during the month. It provides columns
for the date, RCD number, JEV number, name of collecting officer,
collections (debit and credit), deposits (debit and credit) and sundry
columns in the collections and deposits columns (debit and credit).
As a result of the foregoing deficiency, we could not trace specific collections to
the CRJ and the accountabilities of collectors for collections and deposits could not be
validated and thus, we were precluded from verifying the propriety and accuracy of the
journal entries made.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant henceforth prepare
separate JEV for each RCD under GF and SEF in accordance with Section 34 of the
NGAS Manual Volume I to ensure recording of financial transactions in the
journals in time sequence.
The Municipal Accountant agreed to implement the audit recommendation this
year 2019.

In the review of source documents, the said bank deposits could not be traced to
particular collections because the Municipal Treasurer reported them separately using the
format for RD instead of RCD. This practice is contrary to the following provisions of the
NGAS Manual:
a. Section 49, Volume II:
Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD). – The Report of Collections
and Deposits shall be prepared by the following:
3. Treasurer/Cashier – for collections received from liquidating officers,
taxpayers and other sources and deposits to depository banks.
b. Section 52, Volume II:
Report of Disbursements (RD). – The Report of Disbursements shall be
reported by the Accountable/Disbursing Officers to support the liquidation
of their cash advances. The report shall be certified as to correctness by the
Disbursing Officer and forwarded to the Accounting Unit for the
preparation of the JEV.
35
c. Section 29, Volume I:
Reporting for Collections and Deposits. – Collectors/tellers shall issue a
receipt to acknowledge collections made. The receipt maybe in the form
of pre-numbered Official Receipts, or cash tickets and the like. At the
close of each business day, these collectors/tellers shall accomplish the
Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) in four copies. The original
and two copies, together with the duplicates of the official receipts issued,
shall be submitted to the treasurer/cashier to whom the cash collected shall
be turned over. The fourth copy of the RCD shall be retained by the
collector/teller concerned. XXX
d. Section 32, Volume I:
Deposit of Collections. – The Treasurer/Cashier shall deposit intact all
his collections as well as all collections turned over to him by the
collectors/tellers with the authorized depository bank daily or not later
than the next banking day. He shall record all deposits made in the
cashbook and prepare the RCD.
We recognized the geographic location and distance from the nearest Authorized
Government Depository Bank (AGDB) as a primary constraint faced by the Municipality
of Cuyo with regard to its bank transactions. Travelling takes around 15 to 30 hours per
trip, provided that the weather allows it at all. For this reason, the Municipal Treasurer
finds it impractical to make daily or weekly bank deposit of collections.
However, the said constraint doesn’t warrant non-conformance with the use of
proper accounting forms in reporting collections and deposits. The Municipal Treasurer
should accomplish Section A-2 of the RCD whenever collections are turned over to her
by accountable officers and Section B upon deposit of the same. This way, deposits can
be properly matched to collections to show that they were all intact.
The use of RDs in reporting bank deposits resulted in misleading information in
the municipal records. Collections were not properly matched to deposits, thereby
creating opportunity for the Municipal Treasurer to disregard prompt and complete
deposits of collections.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer henceforth report all bank
deposits in the RCD in accordance with Section 32, Volume I and Section 49,
Volume II of the NGAS Manual in order to check proper matching of collections
and deposits.
The Municipal Treasurer agreed to implement the audit recommendation this year
2019.

1.6 Cash in Local Treasury was subjected to incorrect practices of the Municipal
Treasury Office, particularly not depositing cash collections intact to the bank and
using them for cash disbursements contrary to Sections 28 and 45 of COA Circular
No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992, thereby exposing cash to the risk of loss and/or
misuse.
Under the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units as prescribed
under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, the Cash in Local Treasury
Account had the following description:
This account is used to record the amount of cash received by the

(AGDB). Credit this account for deposits to AGDBs.

receipt of cash by the Local Treasurer; and (2) deposit of cash to the AGDB. Only cash

1.6.2 Cash payments totaling ₱7,732,063.20 were made out of cash collections and
not from a properly drawn cash advance contrary to Section 45 of COA
Circular No. 32-382 thereby exposing cash to risk of misapplication.
According to Section 172, Book III, Volume I of the Government
Accounting and Auditing Manual (GAAM), ideally, cash should be handled under
44
the general principles of the Imprest System where all payments must be made by
check and only payments in small amounts may be made in cash through the petty
cash fund. In practice, however, there are certain instances when it may be very
difficult, impractical or impossible to make payments by check. In such cases,
cash payments are allowed through cash advance but subject to limitations.
Regular and Special Cash Advances were defined under Section 3 of COA
Circular No. 97-002 as follows:
3.1 Regular cash advances are those granted to cashiers, disbursing
officers, paymasters, and/or property/supply officers for any of the
following purposes:
3.2.1 Salaries and Wages
3.2.2 Commutable allowances
3.2.3 Honoraria and other similar payments to officials and
employees
3.2.4 Petty operating expenses consisting of small payments for
maintenance and operating expenses which cannot be paid
conveniently by check or are required to be paid
immediately.
3.2 Special cash advances are those granted on the explicit authority of
the Head of the Agency only to duly designated disbursing officers
or employees for other legally authorized purposes, as follows:
3.2.1 Current operating expenditures of the agency field office or
of the activity of the agency undertaken in the field when it
is impractical to pay the same by check, such as:
o Salaries, Wages and Allowances
o Maintenance and other operating expenses
3.2.2 Travel expenditures, including transportation fare, travel
allowance, hotel room/lodging expenses and other expenses
incurred by officials and employees in connection with
official travel.
We have observed that in CY 2018, municipal expenditures totaling
₱7,732,063.20 were paid in cash using undeposited collections contrary to Section
45 of COA Circular No. 32-382 dated July 3, 1992 which provides:
Cash payments shall be made only on duly approved
payrolls/disbursement vouchers/liquidation vouchers out of regular
cash advances or special cash advances.

The Management should have designated a disbursing officer and issued
him/her a regular or special cash advance in accordance with the provisions of
COA Circular No. 97-002. Then, the disbursing officer should have kept a
separate cashbook for the cash advance pursuant to Section 48(q) of COA
Circular No. 92-382 which states that The accountable officer shall maintain an
official cashbook to record the transactions on each cash advance fund.
Cash advances and the disbursements made therefrom should be
accounted for separately from Cash in Local Treasury account. The Revised Chart
of Accounts for Local Government Units as prescribed under COA Circular No.
2015-009 provides the following accounts for cash advances:
46
Account Title: Advances for Operating Expenses
Account Number: 1-03-05-010
Normal Balance: Debit
Description: This account is used to record in the agency books the
amount of advances granted to accountable officers for
payment of operating expenses of operating/field units.
Credit this account upon liquidation.
Account Title: Advances for Payroll
Account Number: 1-03-05-020
Normal Balance: Debit
Description: This account is used to record the amount granted to
regular disbursing officer for payment of salaries, wages,
honoraria, allowances and other personnel benefits. Credit
this account upon liquidation.
Account Title: Advances to Special Disbursing Officer
Account Number: 1-03-05-030
Normal Balance: Debit
Description: This account is used to record the amount granted to
agency’s accountable officers and employees for special
purpose/time-bound undertakings to be liquidated within a
specified period. This includes labor payroll for projects
undertaken by administration. Credit this account upon
liquidation.
Account Title: Advances to Officers and Employees
Account Number: 1-03-05-040
Normal Balance: Debit
Description: This account is used to record amount advanced to officers
and employees for official travel. Credit this account for
liquidation.
Due to the practice of making cash payments out of collections, the
balance of Cash in Local Treasury account represented a mixture of the municipal
collections to be deposited and cash intended for disbursements. This
unnecessarily exposed cash to a high risk of misapplication.
Furthermore, this practice circumvented the rules and regulations on
granting and utilization of cash advances as provided under Section 4 of COA
Circular No. 97-002 which states that:
47
1. No Cash advance shall be given unless for a legally specific
purpose.
2. No additional cash advances shall be allowed to any official or
employee unless the previous cash advance given to him is first
settled or a proper accounting thereof is made.
3. A cash advance shall be reported on as soon as the purpose for
which it was given has been served.
4. Only permanently appointed officials shall be designated as
disbursing officers. Elected officials may be granted a cash
advance only for their official traveling expenses.
5. Only duly appointed or designated disbursing officers may
perform disbursing functions. Officers and employees who are
given cash advances for official travel need not be designated
as Disbursing Officers.
6. Transfer of cash advance from one Accountable Officer (AO)
to another shall not be allowed.
7. The cash advance shall be used solely for the specific legal
purpose for which it was granted. Under no circumstance shall
it be used for encashment of checks or for liquidation of a
previous cash advance.
8. The Accountant shall obligate all cash advances granted. He
shall see that cash advances for a particular year are not used to
pay expenses of other years.
The foregoing audit observation indicated weakness in the internal control
system of the Municipal Treasury Office.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor designate a Disbursing
Officer who shall be in charge of the cash disbursements through regular or
special cash advances drawn in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer henceforth
refrain from making cash payments out of the Cash in Local Treasury.
The Municipal Treasurer agreed to refrain from making cash payments out
of the Cash in Local Treasury collections.

We also reiterated our recommendation that in the case of the deceased accountable officers, the Management should request from COA to write-off the accounts. observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor through its BAC to maintain the accuracy and completeness of the PMR and submit it to the GPPB and to this Office within 14 days at the end of each semester. 

We recommended that the BAC of the Municipality should invite COA representative as Observer in its procurement process especially during eligibility checking, shortlisting, pre-bid conference, preliminary examination of bids, bid evaluation, opening of the bids, and post-qualification. Likewise, the BAC should see to it that at least one observer (1) must come from a duly recognized group in a sector and the other shall come from non-government organization (NGO). The observers shall be invited at least five (5) calendar days before the date of the procurement stage. In the event that a procurement activity has to be postponed, the observers shall be notified immediately of the change in schedule.
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipality should require their bidders to submit to us Sworn Affidavit that it is not related to the HOPE or to the public officers or employees by consanguinity or affinity up to the third civil degree and the same should be submitted to the Audit Team together with the required documentary requirements.
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipality should procure its common-use supplies, materials and equipment (CSEs) under Procurement Service of DBM. If the BAC of the Municipality desires to procure its CSEs other than the said depot on the grounds of efficiency, practicality, economic viability and/or non-availability of stocks in such depot, the procuring entity must secure first a Certificate of Non-Availability of Stocks (CNAS) from such depot.
We recommended that the Municipality should immediately submit to us the requested documents to support its compliance with the pertinent law/s. Non-compliance may result to suspension of the procurement concerned until they have complied with the requirements.
Henceforth, we recommended strict compliance with applicable laws and rules of R.A. No. 9184 and its R-IRR.

with due regard to the existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations.

the General Provisions of R.A. No. 10964 known as the “General Appropriations

provisions of BIR Revenue Regulation No. 2-98 dated April 17, 1998, as amended by
RR Nos. 14-2002 dated September 9, 2002 and 10-2008 dated July 8, 2008 to avoid
possible interest/penalty for late payments and/or possible misuse of the government
funds.

We recommended the Municipality to faithfully comply with the
preparation and submission of the documents as prescribed under DEPED-
DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended the Municipality, through its Municipal Treasurer,

for the Special Education Fund.

R.A. No. 7160 and Section 4 of DEPED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s.

Committee and enjoin the concerned department heads’ compliance with the

and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 for the

observations.recommendation:
6.1 Project Procurement Plan (PPMP)
We recommended the management to prepare PPMP, consolidate it with the APP that must be approved by the Municipal Mayor and submit it to COA office timely, accurately and completely in the prescribed latest format issued by the Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB).

6.2 Procurement Monitoring Report
We recommended the Municipal Mayor through its BAC to maintain the accuracy and completeness of the PMR and submit it to the GPPB and to this Office within 14 days of each semester.

6.3 Observers
We recommended that the Municipality BAC should invite COA representative as Observer. Likewise, the BAC should see to it that at least one observer (1) must come from a duly recognized group in a sector and the other shall come from non-government organization (NGO). The observers shall be invited at least five (5) calendar days before the date of the procurement stage.

6.4 Disclosure of Relations
Thus, we recommended the Municipality to require their contractors/ suppliers to submit to us Sworn Affidavit that it is not related to the HOPE or to the persons mentioned above by consanguinity or affinity up to the third civil degree.

6.5 Procurement Service (PS)
We recommended that the Municipality should procure its common-use supplies, materials and equipment under Procurement Service of DBM. On the grounds of efficiency, practicality, economic viability and/or non-availability of stocks in such depot, the procuring entity must secure a Certificate of Non-Availability of Stocks (CNAS) from such depot. Those CSEs that the LGU already procured in violation of the provisions mentioned above, the management should send us the written explanation that would justify their action. Otherwise, the persons responsible would suffer the corresponding penalty prescribed by law.

Overall Recommendations:
• We recommended the Municipality to uphold and guide themselves with the applicable laws and rules such as R.A. No. 9184 and its R-IRR, respectively.
• Section 4(6) of PD 1445 states that “Claims against government funds shall be supported with complete documentation.” In line with this, we recommend the Municipality to submit accurately, timely and completely the documentary requirements as prescribed by law, or rules and regulations. Section 39(3) of said decree provides “It shall be the duty of the officials or employees concerned xxx to comply promptly with these requirements. Failure or refusal to do so without justifiable cause shall constitute a ground for administrative disciplinary action xxx.”
• Lastly, no public officials or employees should be pecuniary interested, directly or indirectly, in any contract or transaction of the government in violation of the laws such as Code of Conduct and Ethical Standards for Public Officials and Employees (R.A. 6713) and Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act (R.A. 3019). Even if the public officials and employees of the Municipality don’t know the law, they are still liable because Art. 3 of the Civil Code provides “Ignorance of the law excuses no one from compliance therewith.”
